1: vayar Bil'am ki tov be'einei YHWH levarech et Yisra'el velo halakh kefa'am befa'am liqrat nachashim: 'Balaam saw that it was good in YHWH's eyes to bless Israel, so he did not go as at other times to seek (liqrat) omens (nachashim).' This is a turning point: Balaam abandons his professional divination practice. The word nachashim (omens/divination) is from the same root as nachash (serpent) — the snake-omen tradition. Balaam turns instead to face the wilderness where Israel is encamped. The shift from divination to direct divine speech marks the third oracle as qualitatively different from the first two.
2: vayissa Bil'am et einav vayar et Yisra'el shochen liShvatav vattehi alav ruach Elohim: 'He looked out and saw Israel encamped tribe by tribe (liShvatav, by their tribes), and the Spirit of God (ruach Elohim) came upon him.' The Spirit's coming on Balaam — a Gentile diviner — is the only explicit Spirit-inspiration of a non-Israelite prophet in the Pentateuch. The ruach Elohim formula is the same as Gen 1:2 (Spirit hovering over the waters) and Exod 31:3 (Bezalel filled with the Spirit for tabernacle craft). The visual trigger — Israel's tribal camp arrangement, the perfectly ordered sacred community — evokes the Spirit's presence.
3: vayissa meshalow vayomar ne'um Bil'am ben Be'or une'um haggever shetum ha'ayin: The oracle formula intensifies for the third and fourth oracles: ne'um (utterance/oracle, the prophetic formula word) appears twice in this verse. Ne'um is the standard prophetic-word marker (cf. ne'um YHWH, oracle of YHWH, throughout the prophets). Shetum ha'ayin is notoriously difficult: traditionally 'whose eye is opened/unstopped' (from shatam, to be stopped up — the opened man is one whose eye is unstopped); the LXX reads alethinos (true/genuine). The visual faculty emphasized here is prophetic sight — Balaam's eye is the organ of revelation in this oracle.
4: ne'um shome'a imrei El asher machazeh Shaddai yechezeh nopel ugeluy einayim: 'The utterance of one who hears the words of God, who sees a vision (machazeh) from the Almighty (Shaddai), who falls prostrate with eyes wide open (geluy einayim).' The prophetic credential formula is elaborate: (1) hearing God's words, (2) seeing visions from El Shaddai (the patriarchal divine name, Gen 17:1, 28:3), (3) falling prostrate while the eyes remain open. The combination of prostration and open eyes describes an ecstatic visionary state — the prophet is physically overcome but spiritually perceptive. El Shaddai in this context connects Balaam's prophecy to the Abrahamic covenant framework.
5: mah tovu ohalekha Ya'aqov mishkenotekha Yisra'el: 'How beautiful (mah tovu) are your tents (ohalekha), Jacob, your dwelling places (mishkenotekha), Israel!' This verse became the traditional Jewish liturgical greeting sung upon entering a synagogue — Mah Tovu. The irony that Israel's most beautiful liturgical verse was composed by a hired Gentile diviner is noted with delight in Jewish tradition. The parallelism ohalekha/mishkenotekha (tents/dwelling places) and Ya'aqov/Yisra'el (the patriarchal names) is the standard Hebrew poetic chiasm.
6: kinechalim nitayu kegannot alei nahar ka'ahalim nata' YHWH ka'arazim alei mayim: The fourfold simile — spreading valleys, riverside gardens, aloe trees planted by YHWH, cedars beside the waters — develops the beauty motif with lush water imagery. Ahalim (aloe trees) is the aloeswood (Aquilaria), a prized aromatic — one of the most valuable exotic trees in ancient trade. Arazim (cedars) are the towering Lebanon cedars, symbol of majesty and permanence. Israel's community is portrayed as a garden of rare, fragrant, permanent trees — an Eden metaphor.
7: yizzal mayim midalyav vezar'o bemayim rabbim: 'Water will flow from his buckets, his seed will have abundant water.' The agricultural blessing of water abundance is followed by the political-royal oracle: 'his king will be greater than Agag, and his kingdom will be exalted.' The mention of Agag (king of the Amalekites, 1 Sam 15) is proleptic — anticipating the future king who will defeat Amalek. The oracle looks forward to the Davidic monarchy as the fulfillment of the national greatness it predicts.
8: El motsi'o miMitsrayim ketho'afot re'em lo: The wild ox (re'em) image returns from 23:22 — God who brought Israel from Egypt with wild ox strength now acts through Israel's king: 'he will devour hostile nations, crush their bones, and shatter them with his arrows.' The military imagery — devouring, bone-crushing, arrow-shattering — describes Israel as the instrument of divine warfare against the nations. This is conquest language that anticipates the campaigns Israel will conduct under Joshua.
9: kara shakav ka'ari vekalavi mi yiqimenu: 'He crouches and lies down like a lion or a lioness — who dares rouse him?' The lion-at-rest image from 23:24 returns, but now adds the Abrahamic formula: 'Blessed is everyone who blesses you (mevarekheikha varukh) and cursed is everyone who curses you (ore'arekha arur).' This is the verbatim reversal of Balak's initial hope (22:6) and the precise language of Gen 12:3 applied to Israel as a nation. Balaam, hired to curse, delivers the Abrahamic blessing formula over the nation he was meant to curse.
10: vayichar af Balaq el Bil'am vayispoq et kappav: 'Balak was furious with Balaam and struck his palms together' — the clapping/striking of palms is a gesture of outrage or dismissal (cf. Job 27:23; Lam 2:15 where it signals scorn). Balak's final accusation — 'I called you to curse my enemies but you have blessed them three times (shalosh pe'amim)' — summarizes the entire narrative pattern. The counting of three blessings marks the completeness of the reversal: every opportunity for cursing was transformed into blessing.
11: veattah borah lekha el meqomekha amarti kabbed akabedekha vehineh mena'akha YHWH mikkavod: 'Now go back to your home! I intended to honor you greatly, but YHWH has kept that honor from you.' Mena'akha YHWH mikkavod (YHWH has withheld honor from you) — the passive construction makes Balak acknowledge YHWH's role in the outcome, even in his anger. He blames not Balaam but YHWH for the failure of his plan.
12: Balaam's response reminds Balak that he warned the initial messengers that his speech was divinely controlled (22:18). The narrative circles back to the prophetic constraint established at the start — completing a frame around the entire oracle sequence.
13: im yitten li Balaq melo beto kesef vezahav lo ukhal la'avor et pi YHWH la'asot tovah o ra'ah millivi: The financial immunity declaration — even a palace full of silver and gold cannot purchase a curse against Israel. The phrase la'asot tovah o ra'ah millivi (to do good or evil of my own will) is significant: Balaam is not constrained by professional ethics but by divine command. He cannot even produce a voluntary blessing or curse — all his speech is under divine control.
14: veattah hineni holekh le'ammi lekha iy'atsekha asher ya'aseh ha'am hazeh le'ammekha be'acharit hayamim: 'Let me warn you what this people will do to your people in the days to come (be'acharit hayamim).' The phrase be'acharit hayamim (in the latter days/end of days) is the standard prophetic temporal frame for eschatological oracle — it appears in Balaam's famous Star oracle (v17) and in Jacob's deathbed blessings (Gen 49:1). What follows is the most explicitly messianic oracle in Numbers.
15: vayissa meshalow vayomar ne'um Bil'am ben Be'or une'um haggever shetum ha'ayin: The fourth oracle repeats the formula of vv.3-4 almost verbatim — establishing it as a paired companion to the third oracle. The repetition of shetum ha'ayin (whose eye is opened) emphasizes that what follows is revealed vision, not political prediction.
16: ne'um shome'a imrei El veyode'a da'at Elyon machazeh Shaddai yechezeh nopel ugeluy einayim: The fourth oracle credential formula adds veyode'a da'at Elyon (one who knows the knowledge of the Most High). Elyon (Most High) is the divine title associated with pre-Israelite worship in Jerusalem (Gen 14:18-22, Melchizedek's god) and with Psalm 91. The expansion of the credential formula for the fourth oracle — adding Most High knowledge to the third oracle's Spirit-anointing — signals that the fourth oracle is the culminating revelation.
17: er'ennu velo attah ashurennu velo qarov darakh kokhav miYa'aqov veqam shevet miYisra'el: 'I see him, but not now; I behold him, but not near. A Star shall come out of Jacob and a Scepter shall rise from Israel.' This is the primary messianic oracle of Numbers and one of the most important in the entire OT. Key terms: kokhav (star) — celestial body associated with divine messengers and royal destiny; shevet (scepter/rod/tribe) — the ruler's rod that identifies kingship, also Jacob's blessing for Judah (Gen 49:10: 'the scepter shall not depart from Judah'). The temporal markers velo attah...velo qarov (not now...not near) explicitly frame this as long-range prophecy. Simon bar Kokhba (132-135 CE) took his messianic name from this verse. The Magi's star in Matt 2 is read in the church fathers as a fulfillment of Balaam's oracle — the Gentile star-seer from Mesopotamia has Mesopotamian successors who follow the star to the Davidic Messiah.
18: vehayah Edom yerushah vehayah yerushah Se'ir oyevav veYisra'el 'oseh chayil: 'Edom will become a possession, Seir his enemy a possession, but Israel will act valiantly.' Edom (the territory and descendants of Esau/Seir) is the first named target of the Star-Scepter's conquest. The partial fulfillment in David's subjugation of Edom (2 Sam 8:14) is the historical referent; the eschatological reading sees this as the Messiah's complete victory over all enemy nations. The word yerushah (possession, inheritance) is the same root as yarash (to inherit/dispossess) used throughout the conquest narratives.
19: veyerd miYa'aqov vehe'evid sarid me'ir: 'A ruler will come from Jacob and destroy the survivors of the city.' The brevity of this verse intensifies the finality — the ruler's authority extends to total conquest. The phrase sarid me'ir (survivor of the city) may refer to Edom's fortified cities or to the general category of all enemy strongholds. The oracle does not name the Messiah but describes the scope of his rule.
20: vayar et Amaleq vayissa meshalow vayomar reshit goyim Amaleq ve'acharito adei oved: Balaam sees Amalek and delivers the fifth oracle: 'Amalek was first among the nations, but its end is destruction.' Reshit goyim (first of nations) may refer to Amalek's early military prominence (first nation to attack Israel after the Exodus, Exod 17:8-16) rather than chronological priority. The oracle pronounces Amalek's terminal fate — adei oved (unto destruction/perishing), the same root as in Gen 2:15's garden-keeping charge. The Amalek oracle is the dark counterpart to the Israel-blessing oracles.
21: vayar et haQeini vayissa meshalow vayomar eitan moshavekha vesim batsur qinnekha: The Kenite oracle: 'Your dwelling place is secure (eitan, permanent/enduring), your nest (qinekha) is set in rock.' The Kenites (Cain's descendants, associated with Moses's father-in-law Hobab/Jethro) were semi-nomadic metalworkers who lived in rocky terrain. The word qen (nest) is a pun on Qeni (Kenite). Their security is acknowledged before the oracle turns to their destruction.
22: ki im yihyeh levu'er Qayin ad mah Ashshur tishbekha: 'Yet the Kenite will be burned up (boa'er, consumed by fire, from ba'ar) when Asshur takes you captive.' The Kenite oracle has two parts: security (v21) and eventual destruction by Assyria (v22). Asshur (Assyria) as the instrument of destruction connects this to the later Assyrian deportations (8th century BCE) that fulfilled similar prophetic oracles.
23: vayissa meshalow vayomar oy mi yichyeh mishum El eleh: 'Alas, who can survive when God brings these things about?' The seventh oracle begins with the exclamation oy (woe/alas) — the prophetic woe formula. The oracle is brief and oracular about cosmic-scale events that even the speaker does not fully explain.
24: vetsim me'yad Kittim ve'inu Ashshur ve'inu Ever vegam hu adei oved: 'Ships from the coasts of Kittim will oppress Asshur and Eber, but they too will come to ruin.' Kittim in Numbers probably refers to the Mediterranean coastal peoples (Cyprus, later used more broadly for western maritime powers including Greece and Rome in Daniel 11:30). The oracle's historical scope extends to Greek or Roman maritime expansionism — a vision far beyond Balaam's immediate context. The final adei oved (unto destruction) mirrors the Amalek oracle — all the nations' power ends in ruin.
25: vayaqom Bil'am vayelekh vayashov limqomo vegam Balaq halakh ledarko: 'Then Balaam got up and returned to his home, and Balak went his way.' The abrupt ending — two parties simply departing, no farewell, no ceremony — underscores the anti-climax of Balak's enterprise. He came with silver and a professional curse; he leaves with nothing, the curse transformed into blessing. The narrative irony is complete. The final note that Balak 'went his way (ledarko)' closes a narrative in which his intended path (the path of cursing Israel) was blocked at every turn — he returns to his own path, having failed entirely.
